full_response:
The rotation of a point \((x_0, y_0, z_0)\) by an angle \(\theta\) about the origin in the \(xy\)-plane corresponds to a rotation around the \(z\)-axis. This transformation leaves the \(z\)-coordinate unchanged while mixing the \(x\)- and \(y\)-coordinates according to the standard 2D rotation formulas.

The new coordinates are:
\[
x' = x_0 \cos \theta - y_0 \sin \theta, \quad y' = x_0 \sin \theta + y_0 \cos \theta, \quad z' = z_0.
\]

In matrix form, this is:
\[
\begin{pmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ z' \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \cos \theta & -\sin \theta & 0 \\ \sin \theta & \cos \theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x_0 \\ y_0 \\ z_0 \end{pmatrix}.
\]

\boxed{(x_0 \cos \theta - y_0 \sin \theta,\ x_0 \sin \theta + y_0 \cos \theta,\ z_0)}
Confidence: 100%
